semantic_journey: Arabic saqata = to fall/drop, to trickle down (the fundamental motion of descent). Greek stakte = distilled/dripped substance, specifically myrrh distillate (something that has dripped down). The semantic journey: falling/dropping -> the dripped result. Arabic encodes the action of falling/dripping; Greek names the resulting dripped substance. Action-to-result in one step. This is a genuine connection: the concept of something that drips/falls down -> the distilled substance.
key_insight: Arabic falling/dripping (saqata) and Greek dripped substance (stakte) share the dripping-down concept - action to result.

semantic_journey: Arabic nardat = a plant used in perfumery (nard-related plant). Greek narte = a plant used in perfumery (giant fennel, Ferula communis, used in ancient pharmacology and perfumery). Both name plants used in the same context - perfumery/medicine. The Semitic root n-r-d for nard/aromatic plant is attested across Semitic languages and entered Greek as nardon. This appears to be the same aromatic plant term in different morphological forms. Material culture transmission of a perfumery plant name.
key_insight: Arabic perfumery plant (nardat) and Greek perfumery plant (narte) plausibly share an aromatic plant term.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-sandal = sandalwood (fragrant medicinal wood from India/Arabia). Greek sandalon = sandal (footwear). The original reasoning correctly notes both are loanwords from the same Sanskrit/Old Iranian source. However: Arabic borrowed the wood name from Sanskrit (chandana = sandalwood); Greek borrowed the footwear name from Persian/Aramaic (sandal/shoe). These are two DIFFERENT words from Sanskrit/Persian that happen to sound similar - the wood and the shoe are distinct items. Shared loanword from separate Sanskrit/Persian roots that happen to resemble each other.
key_insight: Arabic sandalwood and Greek sandal (footwear) are different loanwords from Sanskrit/Persian - false cognate despite phonetic similarity.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-qalf = bark/peel/husk; pomegranate rind; tree bark; foreskin - the outer encasing layer of something. Greek keluphos = sheath, case, shell, husk, pod - the outer enclosing shell of something. Both directly mean the outer shell/husk/case of something. Arabic encodes the peeling/stripping action and its result; Greek names the outer shell/case. Direct semantic preservation of the outer-enclosing-layer concept. The phonetic match (q-l-f vs k-l-ph) is strong.
key_insight: Arabic outer husk/bark/peel (al-qalf) directly matches Greek outer shell/case (keluphos) - confirmed direct preservation.

semantic_journey: Arabic dalnaz = fat/plump person (heavy body). Greek sandalon = sandal (footwear). A fat person and a sandal are completely unrelated semantic fields. The original scoring appears to be a phonetic accident with no semantic justification. The original reasoning even cites a calibration anchor at 0.65 suggesting this is borderline - but fat person and sandal have no real connection.
key_insight: Arabic fat/plump person and Greek sandal (footwear) are completely unrelated semantic fields - false positive.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-hatt = to put down/lower, to descend. Greek oulokhutai = barley groats poured down in ritual sacrifice (from oulokhutai = ritual barley). The Arabic concept of putting down/lowering is generic; the Greek refers to a specific ritual act of pouring barley. The chain: putting down -> pouring barley in ritual requires knowing: (1) the substance is barley, (2) the context is ritual. Too many unspecified steps. The specific ritual context of the Greek word is not derivable from the Arabic root.
key_insight: Arabic putting down/lowering does not specifically connect to Greek ritual barley-pouring - too many inferential steps.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-qash and Greek Aiolikos = Aeolic (pertaining to the Aeolian Greek dialect). The original reasoning says both involve dialect/language variety. Arabic al-qash has unclear meaning relevant here; Greek Aiolikos is a proper ethnic/linguistic adjective (Aeolian = from Aeolia). This is a proper ethnic/dialect name, not a lexical cognate. Even if Arabic has a word for dialect, Aeolic is a specific proper ethnolinguistic designation.
key_insight: Greek Aiolikos is a proper ethnic-linguistic designation (Aeolian dialect) - not a lexical root cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic hata = strong palm-grasping, enclosing with the hand (the act of firmly taking hold). Greek eikhete = you had/you held (imperfect of ekho = to have/hold). Both encode the concept of holding/grasping. Arabic centers on the physical act of seizing with the palm; Greek preserves the state of having/holding. The core concept of holding/possession is directly shared. One step: palm-grasping -> state of holding/having.
key_insight: Arabic palm-grasping/enclosing and Greek to-have/hold share the core holding/possession concept.
